pr.title: Fix copy paste mistake on postprocess.py
pr.author: PsiLupan

summary: Fixed a one-line copy/paste bug in tools/postprocess.py inside impl_postprocess_elf. When collecting mr_epilogues, the script previously appended (lwz_pos, it), but the surrounding condition was checking both lwz_pos and mr_pos; the fix correctly appends (lwz_pos, mr_pos). This keeps the postprocess epilogue tuple consistent with the detected lwz/mr positions rather than using the current iterator value.

what_changed.details: In impl_postprocess_elf, changed mr_epilogues.append((lwz_pos, it)) to mr_epilogues.append((lwz_pos, mr_pos)) when both lwz_pos and mr_pos are present.

smart_moves: The fix was minimal and targeted: it changed only the incorrect tuple element, preserving the surrounding epilogue-detection logic.
The corrected value matches the condition that guards the append, making the recorded mr_epilogues tuple use the actual detected mr instruction position.
decomp_lessons: Small tooling bugs in postprocessing scripts can affect generated or fixed-up assembly artifacts, so copy/paste tuple fields should be checked against the variables used in nearby detection conditions.
When a postprocess helper records paired instruction positions, the tuple should store the semantically named positions, not a loop iterator unless the iterator is explicitly the intended location.
naming_conventions: Position variables in tools/postprocess.py use short instruction-derived names such as addi_pos, mtlr_pos, lwz_pos, and mr_pos.
The epilogue collection for mr-based patterns is named mr_epilogues.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: The tooling scans for instruction-position patterns related to function epilogues, including addi/mtlr and lwz/mr combinations.
For the lwz/mr epilogue case, the stored tuple should pair lwz_pos with mr_pos, preserving the detected instruction locations for later processing.
searchable_terms: Fix copy paste mistake on postprocess.py
follow_up_queries: Search later PRs for mr_epilogues to see whether this tuple shape was reused or further adjusted.
Inspect tools/postprocess.py around impl_postprocess_elf for other append calls where a loop iterator may have been accidentally used instead of a named instruction position.
